summary: Gruber argues that Apple's decision to switch from Google Maps to its own mapping service in iOS 6 was strategically timed, not premature. With the Google Maps license expiring mid-2013—halfway through iOS 6's expected lifecycle—Apple faced a choice: switch now in a major release, or risk being forced into a mid-cycle swap under worse negotiating conditions. Waiting would have given Google maximum leverage in contract renegotiations. Gruber notes that Apple's mapping acquisitions dating back to 2009 made this transition inevitable, and Google shouldn't have been surprised. The piece reframes the widely criticized Maps launch as the least-bad timing option available to Apple.
key_points: Apple's existing Google Maps deal was set to expire around mid-2013, which would have fallen midway through the iOS 6 lifecycle, creating an untenable situation.
Major platform changes like a mapping backend swap should only ship in major OS releases, not mid-cycle .x updates, making iOS 6.0 the logical moment.
Waiting to negotiate would have given Google enormous leverage, since Apple would either have to accept Google's terms or scramble to replace Maps mid-cycle.
Apple's mapping acquisitions starting in 2009 (Placebase, Poly9, C3 Technologies) made the eventual break from Google Maps obvious and inevitable.
The effective deadline for Apple's decision was months before the deal expired—strategic timing required acting early, not waiting until their backs were against the wall.
money_quotes: If Apple had stuck with Google Maps for another year they would have been forced to renegotiate with Google in a situation where both sides at the table would know that Apple either (a) had to agree to whatever terms Google demanded to extend the deal; or (b) would be forced to swap the mapping back-end of iOS 6 midway through its development cycle.

However tumultuous a change this has been in iOS 6.0, it would have proven more tumultuous and controversial if Apple had been forced by failed contract negotiations to squeeze it into a 6.1 or 6.2 update come May.
Apple wasn't going to wait to negotiate until their backs were to the wall with the currently-shipping version of iOS reliant on Google Maps when the old deal expired.
Big changes come in the major release; bug fixes, security updates, and minor improvements come in post-major-release .x updates.
What exactly did Google think Apple was acquiring these companies for if not to replace Google Maps with their own offering?
key_insight: Apple's controversial Maps launch wasn't premature—it was the strategically necessary moment to avoid being trapped in a mid-cycle renegotiation where Google held all the leverage.
